topics.title: What APQP Is and the Five Phases
topics.summary: Advanced Product Quality Planning (APQP) is a structured framework to develop a product and its process so quality is built in up front, not inspected in later. It runs in five phases: (1) Plan & Define Program — turn customer voice into design goals; (2) Product Design & Development — DFMEA, design reviews, prototypes; (3) Process Design & Development — PFMEA, process flow, floor layout; (4) Product & Process Validation — production trial run, MSA, capability, PPAP; (5) Feedback, Assessment & Corrective Action — reduce variation, lessons learned. It front-loads effort to catch problems when changes are cheap.
key_points: APQP builds quality in during planning instead of inspecting it in afterward.
Phase 1 Plan & Define; Phase 2 Product Design & Development; Phase 3 Process Design & Development; Phase 4 Product & Process Validation; Phase 5 Feedback & Corrective Action.
DFMEA lives in Phase 2; PFMEA and process flow in Phase 3.
Phase 4 includes the significant production run, MSA, capability, and PPAP.
Front-loading effort = cheaper changes early vs. expensive fixes in production.

flashcards.a: 1 Plan & Define, 2 Product Design & Development, 3 Process Design & Development, 4 Product & Process Validation, 5 Feedback/Assessment/Corrective Action.

flashcards.q: In which APQP phase does PPAP occur?
What is the core philosophy of APQP?
flashcards.a: Build quality in during planning (prevention) rather than inspect it in later (detection).

quiz.q: The DFMEA is primarily developed in which APQP phase?
quiz.explanation: Design FMEA is part of Product Design & Development (Phase 2).

choices: Build quality in up front through planning
quiz.explanation: APQP front-loads planning so quality is designed in, not inspected in.

media.image_caption: The five phases of APQP, gated from planning to corrective action

video.title: APQP Explained: Advanced Product Quality Planning

media.web_notes: APQP runs in five gated phases — Plan & Define, Product Design, Process Design, Product & Process Validation, and Feedback/Corrective Action — each with required deliverables. The core tools (DFMEA, PFMEA, Control Plan, MSA, SPC, PPAP) are produced across these phases, not bolted on at the end.

topics.title: PPAP — Production Part Approval Process
topics.summary: PPAP is the standardized package a supplier submits to prove it can make conforming parts at the required rate before mass production is approved. It bundles evidence — design records, DFMEA/PFMEA, process flow, control plan, MSA studies, dimensional results, initial process capability, and more (the classic 18 elements) — summarized on a Part Submission Warrant (PSW). Submission levels 1–5 control how much is sent to the customer (Level 3 is the common default; Level 1 is just the PSW). PPAP answers: 'Do you understand the requirement, and can your process consistently meet it?'
key_points: PPAP = supplier's evidence that it can consistently produce conforming parts at rate.
Built from ~18 elements (design records, FMEAs, control plan, MSA, dimensional, capability, etc.).
Summarized/approved on the Part Submission Warrant (PSW).
5 submission levels; Level 3 is the typical full submission, Level 1 = PSW only.
Required at first production, engineering changes, or process/source changes.

flashcards.q: What is PPAP and what question does it answer?
flashcards.a: Production Part Approval Process — proof a supplier can consistently make conforming parts at rate; it answers 'do you understand and can you meet the requirement?'

flashcards.q: What document summarizes and authorizes a PPAP submission?
Which PPAP submission level is the common default full submission?

quiz.q: The single summary/approval document for a PPAP is the:
quiz.explanation: The PSW is signed to summarize and authorize the PPAP package.

choices: There is a new part, engineering change, or process/source change
quiz.explanation: PPAP triggers on new parts and significant design/process/source changes.

topics.title: The Control Plan
topics.summary: A control plan is a living document that describes how each product/process characteristic will be controlled during production: what is measured, the specification/tolerance, the measurement method and gauge, the sample size and frequency, and the reaction plan if a result is out of limits. It links the PFMEA's identified risks to actual shop-floor controls and ties to the process flow. Control plans exist in three stages: prototype, pre-launch, and production. Special characteristics (safety/critical, often flagged with symbols) get tighter controls.
key_points: Control plan = the documented strategy for controlling each characteristic in production.
For each characteristic: spec, measurement method/gauge, sample size & frequency, reaction plan.
Three stages: prototype → pre-launch → production.
It translates PFMEA risks into concrete shop-floor controls; ties to process flow.
Special/critical characteristics receive enhanced controls.

flashcards.q: What five things does a control plan specify for each characteristic?
flashcards.a: The spec/tolerance, measurement method/gauge, sample size, frequency, and the reaction plan.

flashcards.q: What are the three stages of a control plan?
How does the control plan relate to the PFMEA?
flashcards.a: It turns the PFMEA's identified risks/controls into concrete, ongoing shop-floor controls.

quiz.q: The part of a control plan that says what to do when a measurement is out of tolerance is the:
quiz.explanation: The reaction plan defines the response to a nonconforming result.

quiz.q: Control plans progress through which three stages?
quiz.explanation: Prototype → pre-launch → production are the three control-plan stages.

topics.title: Measurement Systems Analysis (MSA) & Gauge R&R
topics.summary: Before trusting any measurement, you must prove the measurement system itself is good — that's MSA. Total observed variation = actual part variation + measurement system variation. Gauge R&R quantifies the measurement piece: Repeatability (same operator, same part, same gauge — equipment variation) and Reproducibility (different operators measuring the same part — appraiser variation). Guidance: %GRR under 10% is acceptable, 10–30% may be acceptable depending on cost/criticality, and over 30% is unacceptable. Other MSA properties include bias, linearity, and stability.
key_points: Observed variation = part variation + measurement-system variation.
Repeatability = equipment variation (same operator/part/gauge).
Reproducibility = appraiser variation (different operators, same part).
%GRR: <10% good, 10-30% marginal, >30% unacceptable.
Also assess bias, linearity, and stability of the gauge.

flashcards.q: Repeatability vs. reproducibility in Gauge R&R?
flashcards.a: Repeatability = same operator/part/gauge (equipment variation); Reproducibility = different operators on the same part (appraiser variation).

flashcards.q: What %GRR is generally acceptable vs. unacceptable?
Why do MSA before a capability study?
flashcards.a: Observed variation includes measurement error; a bad gauge corrupts the capability result.

quiz.q: Two operators get different readings measuring the same part with the same gauge. This is poor:

quiz.explanation: Above 30% the measurement system is unacceptable and must be improved.

topics.title: The Core Tools Work Together
topics.summary: The automotive 'core tools' are not separate islands — APQP is the umbrella process, and the others plug into its phases. The DFMEA/PFMEA identify risks; the Control Plan turns those risks into production controls; MSA validates the gauges used by those controls; SPC monitors the controlled characteristics over time; and PPAP packages all the evidence for customer approval. A 'special characteristic' identified in design flows through DFMEA → PFMEA → Control Plan → SPC, keeping the critical few under tight, traceable control end to end.
key_points: APQP is the umbrella; FMEA, Control Plan, MSA, SPC, and PPAP are its tools.
Risk flow: DFMEA/PFMEA → Control Plan → MSA validates gauges → SPC monitors → PPAP approves.
Special characteristics are traceable across DFMEA, PFMEA, Control Plan, and SPC.
Each tool feeds the next; gaps between them are where defects escape.

flashcards.q: How do the core tools connect a risk to production control?
flashcards.a: FMEA identifies the risk → Control Plan controls it → MSA validates the gauge → SPC monitors it → PPAP approves the evidence.

flashcards.q: What ties APQP, FMEA, Control Plan, MSA, SPC, and PPAP together?
flashcards.a: APQP is the umbrella process; the others are tools used within its phases, each feeding the next.

quiz.q: A safety-critical 'special characteristic' should be traceable through:
quiz.explanation: Special characteristics flow end-to-end so the critical few stay under traceable control.
